title: Scalars — a single number representing one property
verdict: PASS — all 6 checks pass on the revised corpus. The two prior hard fails are resolved: (1) §5 'Scalars compared to what comes next' now contains exactly one name-and-defer sentence per downstream concept (vectors, matrices) with explicit 'covered later' topic pointers — no structural definitions, no example notation; (2) §7 'Real-World Patterns' no longer contains 'batch size', 'gradient step size', or 'inference' — these have been replaced with plain-language descriptions. One soft finding remains on beginner readability: 'weights' is used in §7 ('updates its weights') without an inline gloss; 'parameters' (the near-synonym) was introduced in topic 6.1, so this does not escalate. Only 1 distinct readability finding category fires; escalation threshold is ≥3. No other soft or hard findings.

section_4_introduction.note: Multi-paragraph intro grounding scalars in 6.1 context.

section_5_core_concepts.note: Four subsections: definition, dimensionless, data pipeline, learning rate/loss, name-and-defer.

section_10_key_takeaways.note: 5 concise takeaways covering the headline concept.

detail.core_concepts_citation_coverage: ~78% of standalone factual assertions in §5–§7 carry citation markers (estimated 14 of 18 assertions)

findings.detail: Used in 'when it updates its weights' without inline gloss. 'Parameters' (near-synonym) was introduced in topic 6.1's concepts_introduced, which mitigates this. Not a hard fail.

prior_hard_fails_resolved.resolution: Replaced with a single name-and-defer sentence: 'when you group several scalars together into an ordered list, you get a vector — you'll explore what that enables and how it works there.' No example notation.
Replaced with a single name-and-defer sentence: 'when you arrange scalars into rows and columns to form a rectangular grid, you get a matrix — that structure and its uses are covered in topic 6.4.'
Replaced with 'the number of examples processed at once'.
Replaced with 'how fast the model adjusts when it updates its weights'.
Replaced with 'when the model makes a prediction'.

length_sanity.note: Word count is at the low end of the atomic range. Not more than 30% outside the floor (floor 1500; 30% below = 1050). Passes. If the builder pads §8 Best Practices, that would add comfort margin.

detail.headline: Scalars — a single number representing one property
detail.scope_check: All subsections stay within scalar territory. Vectors and matrices are named and deferred via single-sentence pointers in §5 and brief references in §4 Introduction (motivational framing, not definitional teaching). No out-of-scope concept is defined or taught at depth.
detail.prior_hard_fails_resolved: Vector structural definition and matrix structural definition in §5 have been cut to name-and-defer one-liners.

terms: weights (§7 line 113, 'updates its weights' — used without gloss; mitigated by 'parameters' being in 6.1 concepts_introduced)
findings.note: Only 1 distinct finding category fires. Escalation to hard fail requires ≥3 distinct finding categories. '32-bit or 64-bit floating-point values' in §5 now has an inline gloss ('stored with high precision, think of it as many decimal places') — resolved from prior report. 'epochs' in §7 has inline gloss ('number of training rounds (epochs)'). Surfaces at HITL #2/#3 for author awareness.

optional_improvements: §7: add a one-phrase gloss for 'weights' (e.g., 'the adjustable numbers the model fine-tunes') so a zero-background learner has full context without needing to recall 6.1.
§5: word count is at the floor of the atomic range (~1510 words); consider expanding §8 Best Practices by 1–2 sentences for margin.
